She refused what she called the white gaze, writing Black experience as the norm and never translating it for outsiders, and in 1993 became the first Black woman to win the Nobel Prize in Literature. By wide agreement she ranks among the greatest American novelists and a towering figure of African-American letters of her century.

title: William Faulkner
summary: The American novelist who turned one small Mississippi county into the whole human heart. From the segregated Deep South, William Faulkner invented Yoknapatawpha County and peopled it across generations to anatomize the region's burden of slavery, defeat, and memory, writing The Sound and the Fury (1929), As I Lay Dying (1930), Light in August (1932), and Absalom, Absalom! (1936) in some of the most demanding modernist prose in the language. He won the Nobel Prize in 1949 and is widely regarded as one of the supreme writers of the American South. Few white writers of his era looked harder at the moral wreckage of slavery, or gave Black characters more interior weight — yet his own public stance on civil rights was that of a gradualist who urged the movement to go slow, and his intemperate remarks during the integration crisis remain hard to defend.

title: The Bill of Rights
summary: The first ten amendments to the United States Constitution, drafted largely by James Madison and ratified in 1791 as the price of the Constitution's acceptance. They wrote into supreme law the freedoms of speech, religion, press, and assembly, the protections of people accused of crimes, and the principle that even a government of the people must be fenced away from its citizens' liberties. When ratified the guarantees did not protect the enslaved, women, or Native peoples, and for most of American history they restrained only the federal government. It was the Fourteenth Amendment and twentieth-century court decisions that slowly turned these words into shields against the states, and several provisions remain bitterly contested today.

title: The Declaration of Independence
summary: The 1776 document by which thirteen British colonies announced their separation from Britain and justified it to the world. Drafted chiefly by Thomas Jefferson and adopted by the Second Continental Congress, it declared that all men are created equal and hold rights no government may take away, and that governments rest on the consent of the governed. Its philosophy was Enlightenment natural rights, owing much to John Locke and to wider sources. Its author enslaved hundreds of people, its Congress struck out a passage condemning the slave trade, and its text smeared Native peoples as savages. Yet its words outran their authors: abolitionists, the women of Seneca Falls, Lincoln, anti-colonial movements, and Martin Luther King all turned the promise of equality against the powers that withheld it.

title: The Federalist Papers
summary: Eighty-five newspaper essays written by Alexander Hamilton, James Madison, and John Jay under the shared pen name Publius in 1787 and 1788, urging New Yorkers to ratify the new United States Constitution. They were partisan campaign documents written to win a vote, not neutral philosophy, and they answered the Anti-Federalists, who feared the new national government would crush the states and liberty. Yet two of them — Federalist No. 10 on faction and the large republic, and No. 51 on the separation of powers — became one of the most influential explanations ever written of how the American government was meant to work. Over time The Federalist became the classic guide to the Constitution's design and a source the Supreme Court mines for the framers' original meaning, an authority its authors could not have foreseen.

title: The Reconstruction Amendments
summary: The Thirteenth, Fourteenth, and Fifteenth Amendments to the United States Constitution, ratified between 1865 and 1870 in the years after the Civil War. Together they abolished slavery, made persons born or naturalized in the United States, and subject to its jurisdiction, citizens entitled to due process and the equal protection of the laws, and barred denying the vote on the basis of race. They turned the federal government from a distant power into the guarantor of rights against the states, rewriting what the Constitution was for. Almost at once the Supreme Court read them narrowly and white-supremacist states defied them, leaving the promise unenforced for nearly a century. The civil rights revolution of the mid-twentieth century revived them, and they are now seen as a second founding, the constitutional core of American equality.

title: Silent Spring
summary: Rachel Carson's 1962 book, in which a marine biologist showed an America where pesticides had silenced the songbirds — building a meticulous, lyrical case that the synthetic chemicals sprayed to conquer nature, above all DDT, were moving through the food chain and poisoning the whole web of life, including humanity itself. Widely credited with launching the modern environmental movement, vindicated by a presidential science panel against a furious and often sexist industry campaign, and tied to the 1972 US DDT ban and the creation of the EPA — though the later near-total bans carry a debated tradeoff for malaria control that Carson, who sought regulation rather than prohibition, never called for.

title: The Souls of Black Folk
summary: W.E.B. Du Bois's 1903 book of fourteen essays that named the wound of being Black in America — declaring that 'the problem of the twentieth century is the problem of the color line,' giving the world the idea of 'double consciousness' and the 'veil,' and openly challenging Booker T. Washington's accommodation with a demand for full civil rights, the vote, and higher education led by a 'Talented Tenth.'

title: The U.S. Constitution
summary: The 1787 document that frames the government of the United States, the oldest written national constitution still in force. Drafted in a single Philadelphia summer to replace the failing Articles of Confederation, it created a federal republic in which power is split between the nation and the states and divided among three branches that check one another. It made itself the supreme law of the land, set a deliberately hard amendment process used only twenty-seven times, and was ratified in 1788. Its genius is structural and its flaw is moral: to win the slave states, the framers built protections for slavery into the design without naming it, through the three-fifths clause, the slave-trade clause, and the fugitive-slave clause, and over-weighted small and slave states through the Senate and the Electoral College. It took the Civil War and later amendments to begin correcting that bargain, and Americans still argue over whether to read the text by its framers' meaning or as a living document.

title: Mass Incarceration
summary: The roughly fivefold rise of the United States prison and jail population from the early 1970s, until a country with a small share of the world's people held a large share of its prisoners, with one of the highest incarceration rates on earth and stark racial disparities. Treated here as a policy question rather than a settled story, it forces an argument that remains open among serious scholars: how much of the rise was driven by a real surge in crime, how much by deliberate policy choices such as the War on Drugs, mandatory minimums, and 'tough on crime' politics, and how much the racial gaps reflect enforcement rather than other causes. Widely seen as one of the central domestic-policy failures and moral questions of modern America, its scale is undisputed while its causes and remedies are fiercely argued.

title: Redlining
summary: The practice, formalized by the federal government in the 1930s, of marking Black and immigrant neighborhoods as too risky to lend in while steering government-backed home loans toward new white suburbs. Treated here as a policy question, it asks how a constitutional democracy built racial inequality into the ordinary machinery of mortgage lending, and why the color drawn on Depression-era maps still shows up, generations later, in the wealth, segregation, and even the health of American cities. Outlawed by the Fair Housing Act of 1968, its consequences remain measurable and its exact mechanism still debated.

title: Common Law
summary: A legal system in which much of the law is made by judges deciding individual cases rather than only by lawmakers writing codes. Its engine is precedent (stare decisis): a court's ruling on a point of law binds later courts in similar cases, so the law grows case by case out of accumulated decisions. Born in England after 1066 and carried across the world by the British Empire, the common law today governs the legal systems of the United States, Canada, Australia, India, and much of Africa and the Caribbean — often imposed on colonized peoples over their own traditions. It is one of the two major Western-derived legal families of the modern world, set against the civil-law systems of Europe and Latin America that build the law from comprehensive codes.

title: Hip-Hop
summary: The culture born in the early 1970s among poor Black and Latino youth in the burning, abandoned South Bronx, who turned two turntables, a microphone, a spray can, and their own bodies into a new art form. Classically described as four elements -- DJing, MCing or rapping, b-boying, and graffiti -- it grew from block parties where DJs looped the breaks of funk records, made sampling one of the central techniques of popular music, spread from one neighborhood to the whole planet, and became by the twenty-first century commercially dominant in the United States and one of the most influential popular musics in the world, carrying within it long arguments over conscious versus commercial rap, misogyny and violence versus reportage of inner-city life, and authenticity versus the corporate industry it became.

title: Jazz
summary: The American music born around 1900 in Black New Orleans, where the blues, ragtime, spirituals, and the brass-band tradition fused into something new built on improvisation and swing -- a propulsive, off-the-beat feel -- and call-and-response. Carried up the river and out across the country by the Great Migration, it became the soundtrack of the twentieth century: the soloist's art of Louis Armstrong, the composerly grandeur of Duke Ellington, the popular swing of the big-band era, the fast and uncommercial bebop of Charlie Parker, and the cool, modal, and free explorations of Miles Davis and John Coltrane. Widely called America's classical music and one of its most influential cultural exports, jazz carried Black genius into concert halls and the global imagination even as its Black creators were segregated, underpaid, and exploited by a white-run industry, and it remains an art of individual freedom inside collective form.

title: Rock and Roll
summary: Black-rooted music that crossed the color line and reshaped youth culture worldwide. Born in the early 1950s when Black rhythm and blues was electrified, sped up, and broke out of segregated radio into the white mainstream, rock and roll was built largely by Black artists -- Chuck Berry, Little Richard, Fats Domino, and the gospel guitarist Sister Rosetta Tharpe -- out of the blues and gospel, fused with country and driven by the electric guitar and a hard backbeat. It gave teenagers their own rebellious sound, terrified parents and authorities, and launched the rock tradition that dominated popular music for half a century. Yet a segregated industry channeled the greatest fame and fortune to white performers, above all Elvis Presley, even as the music's Black creators were too often written out of its story, a credit now more honestly returned to them.

title: The Blues
summary: The foundational Black American music, forged around 1900 among Black people in the rural Deep South, above all the Mississippi Delta, out of the work songs, field hollers, and spirituals carried out of slavery into the world of sharecropping and Jim Crow. Built on bent blue notes, a three-line AAB lyric, call-and-response, and the twelve-bar form, it put individual feeling -- loss, desire, trouble, endurance -- at the center and found beauty and defiance in suffering. It traveled with the Great Migration from the Delta of Charley Patton, Son House, and the legendary Robert Johnson to the electrified Chicago blues of Muddy Waters and Howlin' Wolf, and it became the seedbed from which jazz, rhythm and blues, rock and roll, soul, and much of hip-hop all grew, the common grammar of American popular music.

title: The Witch Hunts
summary: The wave of witch trials that swept Christian Europe and its colonies from roughly 1450 to 1750, in which courts prosecuted people for an imaginary crime — a pact with the Devil to work harm by magic. Local grudges, misfortune, and fear produced accusations; torture produced confessions and named further suspects; and tens of thousands of people, most of them women, were executed. The death toll has been badly mythologized: serious historians estimate roughly 40,000 to 60,000 executions, not the 'nine million' of popular legend.

title: J. Robert Oppenheimer
summary: The American theoretical physicist who led the scientific work of the Manhattan Project at Los Alamos, building the first nuclear weapons — used weeks later on Hiroshima and Nagasaki — and who spent much of the rest of his life warning about what he had helped make: opposing a crash program for the hydrogen bomb, urging international control, and then being stripped of his security clearance in a 1954 hearing widely condemned as a political show trial, a decision the United States government formally vacated in 2022.

title: John von Neumann
summary: The Hungarian-American polymath who helped invent both the modern computer and the logic of nuclear war — giving quantum mechanics its mathematical foundation, co-founding game theory, contributing the implosion mathematics behind the plutonium bomb, and writing the stored-program design that became the blueprint for nearly every computer since.

title: Mark Twain
summary: The American writer and humorist, born Samuel Clemens in 1835, who taught American literature to speak in an American voice. A printer, Mississippi steamboat pilot, Western miner, and journalist before he became the most famous author and lecturer in the country, he made the plain, funny, regional speech of ordinary people into literature. His masterpiece, Adventures of Huckleberry Finn (1885), follows a poor white boy and Jim, an escaped enslaved man, down the river, turning a comic adventure into a deep moral reckoning with slavery and conscience. The book is widely read as a profound anti-slavery satire and is also among the most challenged in American schools for its relentless use of a racial slur and its treatment of Jim. Often called the father of American literature, Twain wrote a humor that carried a blade, turned bitterly anti-imperialist and pessimistic in his later years, and died in 1910 as the return of Halley's Comet lit the sky he had been born under.

title: Nikola Tesla
summary: The Serbian-American inventor whose alternating-current induction motor and polyphase system won the 'War of Currents' and became central to how the world is wired — a real genius of the electrical age whose later wireless dreams went unrealized, who died broke and alone, and who is now half-buried under a modern internet legend.

title: Richard Feynman
summary: The American theoretical physicist who shared the 1965 Nobel Prize for quantum electrodynamics, invented the Feynman diagrams that became one of the standard languages of particle physics, worked on the Manhattan Project as a young man, and — through his lectures, his memoirs, and his televised diagnosis of the Challenger disaster — became the twentieth century's most famous example of science as joyous, skeptical play.

title: Thomas Edison
summary: The American inventor and businessman whose Menlo Park laboratory became the first private laboratory built to produce patentable commercial inventions — an 'invention factory' that produced the phonograph, an early motion-picture system, and, most consequentially, the first practical incandescent light and the whole electrical system to power it, including the Pearl Street central station of 1882. Edison did not invent the light bulb alone; his genius was as a systems-builder and organizer who industrialized invention itself, and he was also a ruthless competitor whose camp waged the cruel propaganda of the War of Currents against alternating current.

title: Hollywood
summary: Both a neighborhood of Los Angeles and, far more importantly, the system of the American film and later television industry centered there. Filmmakers moved west in the 1910s for sunshine, cheap land, and distance from Edison's patent enforcers. By the studio-system decades of the 1920s through the 1940s, a handful of vertically integrated studios, many founded by Jewish immigrant entrepreneurs, controlled production, distribution, and theaters, and turned out films and stars on an assembly line. Hollywood industrialized storytelling into a business of stars, genres, and spectacle, became the dominant force in world cinema, and sold an image of America and of desire, heroism, and happiness to the entire planet. Its record is shadowed: the Production Code censored content for decades, the 1950s blacklist destroyed careers during the Red Scare, and the industry has a long, documented history of racial and gender exclusion and stereotyping, and of off-screen abuse.

title: Popol Vuh
period: Alphabetic K'iche' text c. 1550s; copied by Francisco Ximénez c. 1701–1703
summary: The sacred narrative of the K'iche' Maya of highland Guatemala, telling how the gods shaped the world, failed to make beings who could praise them out of mud and then wood, and at last formed true humans from maize. At its heart lies the long saga of the Hero Twins, Hunahpu and Xbalanque, who descend into the underworld of Xibalba and defeat its lords of death through cunning. The book also records K'iche' history and royal lineage. The original hieroglyphic tradition is lost; the text survives only because K'iche' authors wrote it in their own language using the Latin alphabet around the 1550s, and because a Spanish friar, Francisco Ximénez, copied that manuscript around 1701. It is one of the greatest surviving works of Indigenous American literature and a living sacred narrative for K'iche' Maya communities.

title: The Monroe Doctrine
summary: The principle of United States foreign policy stated by President James Monroe in his annual message to Congress in December 1823. Its core claims were that the Americas were no longer open to future European colonization, that the United States would treat any European attempt to extend its political system into the Western Hemisphere as a threat, and that in return the United States would stay out of Europe's internal quarrels. Spoken when the United States was militarily weak, the warning was largely a bluff that the British navy actually enforced. As the United States grew strong, the same words were reread as a license for its own dominance: the 1904 Roosevelt Corollary claimed a right to police the hemisphere, and Washington used the Doctrine to justify repeated interventions. To many Latin Americans, "America for the Americans" came to mean "Latin America for the United States."

title: Bauhaus
summary: The radical German art-and-design school founded by the architect Walter Gropius in Weimar in 1919, which in just fourteen years fused fine art, craft, and the machine into a new creed: that everything from a teapot to a skyscraper should be honest, functional, and made for the modern age. Born in the ruins of the First World War, it taught painting, furniture, metalwork, weaving, typography, theater, and architecture together, with a faculty that included Paul Klee, Wassily Kandinsky, and Laszlo Moholy-Nagy. Its core creed -- a functionalist principle it summarized as "form follows function" (a phrase it borrowed from Louis Sullivan), that good design strips away ornament to serve use, and that well-made objects mass-produced could improve everyday life -- became the foundation of modern design. The Nazis, who hated its modernist and leftist spirit, forced it to close in 1933, scattering its masters across the world. Its principles became the glass-and-steel International Style and the basis of modern graphic, product, and furniture design, though critics later charged that this style hardened into a cold, placeless corporate orthodoxy.

title: Impressionism
summary: The French art movement of the 1860s through the 1880s in which a band of Paris outsiders -- Claude Monet, Pierre-Auguste Renoir, Edgar Degas, Camille Pissarro, Berthe Morisot, and others -- broke with the official Salon and its rules. Many painted outdoors, with quick visible brushstrokes and bright unmixed color, chasing the fleeting effects of light and atmosphere on the modern world rather than grand historical or religious scenes. Mocked at first as unfinished daubs -- the name itself began as an insult drawn from Monet's Impression, Sunrise -- they staged their own independent exhibitions from 1874 and, helped by new technology like the portable paint tube and the pressure of photography, are widely remembered as having opened the door to modern art.

title: Surrealism
summary: The artistic and literary movement launched in Paris in 1924 by the poet Andre Breton, whose Surrealist Manifesto declared reason a failure and set out to liberate the unconscious mind. Born from the nihilist anti-art revolt of Dada and from Sigmund Freud's theory of the unconscious, it argued that if reason and order had produced the slaughter of the First World War, then a truer reality lay in dreams, desire, chance, and the irrational. Writers practiced automatic writing, composing without conscious control; painters split into two streams, the hallucinatory dream-worlds of Salvador Dali, Rene Magritte, and Max Ernst, and the abstract, biomorphic automatism of Joan Miro and Andre Masson. Its devices, the uncanny juxtaposition, the melting clock, the bowler-hatted everyman, the exquisite corpse, made the familiar strange, and it was always also a revolutionary movement entangled with communism. It spread worldwide into film, photography, and beyond Europe, and helped influence Latin American magical realism among other currents. It carried darker features too: Breton ruled it with an authoritarian hand and expelled those who strayed; its women artists were long treated as muses rather than masters; and Dali's embrace of Franco and self-promotion got him cast out. Widely regarded as one of the most influential movements of the twentieth century, its logic is now woven through advertising, film, and everyday language.

title: The Habsburg Empire
summary: The dynastic empire that the Habsburg family gathered across six centuries, as much by marriage and inheritance as by war, until it ruled the heart of Europe — a multiethnic patchwork of a dozen peoples held together by the dynasty, the Catholic Church, the army, and the bureaucracy, which finally broke apart in 1918 on the force of nationalism and left a fractured Central Europe behind.

title: The Holy Roman Empire
summary: The eight-century political contraption at the heart of Europe that claimed to inherit ancient Rome and the blessing of God, but was really a loose patchwork of hundreds of territories, mostly German but reaching at times into Italy, the Low Countries, and Bohemia, under an elected emperor with little central power — mocked by Voltaire as "neither holy, nor Roman, nor an empire," yet the durable frame of Central European law, dynasty, and identity for eight centuries.

title: The Italian City-States
summary: The rich, fiercely independent cities of late-medieval and Renaissance Italy — above all Florence, Venice, Genoa, and Milan — whose banking, trade, and civic rivalry funded the explosion of art and ideas we call the Renaissance, taught a Florentine clerk named Machiavelli to write about power as it truly works, and whose fatal disunity left Italy easy prey for foreign invasion after 1494.

title: The Prussian State
summary: The small, sandy north-German kingdom that, through relentless discipline, an outsized army, and a famously efficient bureaucracy, punched far above its weight, forged modern Germany under its rule, and gave the world both the model of the capable state and the word "Prussian" as a byword for militarism.

title: The Russian Empire
summary: The vast, cold, autocratic realm that grew from a Moscow principality into the largest contiguous land empire of its age, dragged itself toward Western power by force from above, rested on the bound labor of its serfs, and collapsed in 1917 under the weight of all it had refused to reform.

title: The Vikings
summary: The seafaring Norse people of Scandinavia who, for nearly three centuries, burst out of the north in their superb longships to raid monasteries, found cities, settle Iceland and Greenland, reach North America, carve out states from Russia to Normandy, and trade in slaves and silver from Baghdad to Newfoundland — far more than the horned barbarians of legend, and far harder to romanticize than the modern myth allows.

title: The Commercial Revolution
summary: The long expansion of European trade and finance between roughly 1100 and 1700, the centuries when Europe shifted from a mostly rural, local, barter-and-feudal economy toward a market economy of towns, money, merchants, and banks. It began with the revival of long-distance trade in the Italian city-states, the fairs of Champagne, and the Hanseatic League, and accelerated with the Age of Exploration's oceanic routes. Along the way merchants and bankers refined, spread, and institutionalized much of the machinery of modern finance — double-entry bookkeeping, bills of exchange, insurance, banking, and the joint-stock company — laying the foundation on which capitalism and the Industrial Revolution would later be built. The same instruments and appetites that built modern finance also financed colonial conquest, plantations, and the Atlantic slave trade.

title: The Congress of Vienna
summary: The peace conference of 1814-1815, hosted by Austria and steered by its foreign minister Metternich, where the powers that had defeated Napoleon redrew the map of Europe on two old principles, legitimacy and the balance of power, restoring monarchies the Revolution had toppled and arranging territory so no single state could dominate again. Conservatives remember it for buying Europe roughly a century without a general war; liberals and nationalists remember it as a graveyard of liberty that handed peoples between thrones and crushed the hopes of freedom and nationhood.

title: The Discovery of DNA's Structure
summary: The 1953 discovery at Cambridge that the DNA molecule is a double helix — two strands twined together whose paired chemical letters spell out and copy the instructions for life. James Watson and Francis Crick built the model, but their leap rested crucially on the X-ray crystallography of Rosalind Franklin, whose key image was shown to Watson without her knowledge. The breakthrough revealed how heredity works at the molecular level and helped found the science of molecular biology.

title: The Dreyfus Affair
summary: The twelve-year scandal, beginning in 1894, in which France convicted a Jewish army officer of treason he did not commit, buried the evidence to protect the army's honor, and then tore itself apart over whether truth or the state mattered more. Captain Alfred Dreyfus was framed on forged documents and condemned in large part because he was Jewish; an officer who found the real traitor was silenced; and the novelist Émile Zola's open letter 'J'Accuse...!' split the nation into two furious camps. Dreyfus was eventually exonerated in 1906. The affair exposed the antisemitism beneath a modern republic, helped define the modern public intellectual, and helped convince Theodor Herzl that Jews would never be safe through assimilation, feeding his turn to political Zionism.

title: The English Civil War
summary: The series of wars fought between 1642 and 1651 between the supporters of King Charles I and the supporters of Parliament — a struggle over whether the king ruled by divine right or was bound by the law, deepened by bitter religious conflict between Puritans and the king's church. Parliament built the disciplined New Model Army, defeated the king, and in January 1649 tried and beheaded him, the first public execution of a reigning English monarch by his own subjects. England briefly became a republic and then a near-dictatorship under Oliver Cromwell before the monarchy was restored in 1660 — but the idea that even a king must answer to the law endured.

title: The Glorious Revolution
summary: The overthrow of England's Catholic king James II in 1688, accomplished by a Dutch invasion under William of Orange that a circle of Protestant English elites had invited, and remembered as the moment the English crown became a creature of Parliament and the law. The 1689 Bill of Rights fixed the terms: a monarch could no longer suspend laws, tax, or keep an army without Parliament's consent, and no Catholic could reign. It is called glorious and bloodless because it spared England a civil war, but that label holds only for England. The same takeover drowned Ireland in a bloody war and began centuries of Protestant ascendancy and Catholic grievance.

title: The Inquisition
summary: The network of ecclesiastical tribunals — some papal, some, like the Spanish Inquisition, crown-controlled — built to find and punish heresy, which for centuries policed what people believed through secret trials, torture, and execution by burning. It was not one body but several: the medieval Inquisition that began around 1184 against heretics like the Cathars, the notorious Spanish Inquisition founded in 1478, and the Roman Inquisition of 1542. The Spanish tribunal terrorized converts from Judaism and Islam, policing the sincerity of their faith. Its death toll became a byword for fanatical slaughter, but its own meticulous records suggest the Spanish Inquisition executed roughly 3,000 to 5,000 people over some 350 years — a real engine of fear far smaller than the legend that grew around it.

title: The Norman Conquest
summary: The 1066 invasion in which William, Duke of Normandy, seized the English throne, defeating and killing King Harold at the Battle of Hastings and being crowned on Christmas Day as William the Conqueror. It was the last successful foreign conquest of England, and it remade the country's ruling class, language, law, and landscape so completely that the result is still audible in modern English. The conquest was brutal, marked by the devastating Harrying of the North, and it left behind both a fused English language and the lasting myth of a free Anglo-Saxon age crushed by a Norman yoke.

title: The Paris Commune
summary: The seventy-two days in the spring of 1871 when the working people of Paris seized their own city and governed it themselves as a radical, democratic commune, before the French national government retook the capital by force and drowned the rising in blood. After France lost a humiliating war to Prussia and Paris endured a long siege, the city rose against a conservative government that tried to disarm it. For about two months the Commune separated church and state, made education free and secular, handed idle workshops to worker cooperatives, capped officials' salaries, and flew the red flag. In the 'Bloody Week' of late May the army crushed it, killing many thousands of Parisians in combat and summary executions — older estimates often range from 10,000 to 20,000, though some recent work argues lower; in its final days the Commune itself shot hostages, including the Archbishop of Paris. The brief experiment became the founding myth of modern revolutionary socialism.

title: The Revolutions of 1848
summary: The wave of revolutions that swept across Europe in 1848, when peoples rose almost everywhere demanding constitutions, nations, and rights, briefly toppled kings and chancellors, and then, within months, saw nearly every revolution crushed and the old order restored. Beginning with risings in Sicily and then Paris in early 1848 and spreading like wildfire to the German states, the Austrian (Habsburg) Empire, the Italian lands, and beyond, the 'Springtime of Peoples' forced rulers to flee or grant concessions, convened parliaments, and lifted censorship. Driven by economic crisis and the ideals of liberalism and nationalism, it looked for a few months as though monarchical Europe might fall. By 1849 the monarchies had recovered their armies and crushed the risings one by one, and the kings came back. Yet the defeated demands of 1848 abolished serfdom in the Habsburg lands, made nationalism the central force of European politics, and put democracy and the rights of workers permanently on the agenda. A.J.P. A famous verdict calls 1848 'the turning point at which modern history failed to turn.'

title: The Wars of Religion
summary: The long, savage series of conflicts that followed the Protestant Reformation's splitting of Western Christianity, in which Catholics and Protestants slaughtered one another across Europe from roughly the 1520s to 1648 — the German wars of the Reformation, the French Wars of Religion, the Dutch Revolt, and the catastrophic Thirty Years' War. The killing ended not in the victory of one faith but in exhaustion. Many historians argue that out of that exhaustion came a gradual loosening of religious uniformity and the principle of state sovereignty, partly settled at the Peace of Westphalia in 1648.

title: Ada Lovelace
summary: The English mathematician who, working with Charles Babbage's never-built Analytical Engine, published in 1843 what many call the first algorithm written for a machine — and who grasped, more clearly than almost anyone around Babbage, that such a machine could manipulate not just numbers but symbols, music, and ideas, anticipating the general-purpose computer a century before working computers existed.

title: Antoine Lavoisier
summary: The French chemist who helped transform chemistry into a quantitative science by weighing everything before and after a reaction — establishing the law of conservation of mass, naming oxygen and overthrowing the phlogiston theory of combustion, and co-creating modern chemical nomenclature — before being guillotined in 1794 as a former royal tax collector during the Terror of the French Revolution.

title: Archimedes
summary: The Greek mathematician and engineer of Syracuse, among the greatest of antiquity, who measured the circle by trapping it between polygons, found the area and volume of curved figures by a method of exhaustion that anticipated the integral calculus, founded the sciences of statics and hydrostatics with the law of the lever and the principle of buoyancy, devised the water-raising screw and compound pulley, and was killed by a Roman soldier during the sack of Syracuse in 212 BC.

title: Carl Friedrich Gauss
summary: The German mathematician often called the Prince of Mathematicians, who reshaped number theory, geometry, astronomy, and the science of measurement. Born poor in Brunswick in 1777, Gauss published the 'Disquisitiones Arithmeticae' at twenty-four, computed where the newly discovered Ceres would reappear, gave least squares a powerful theoretical foundation by linking it to the normal error curve, and worked out results he kept private for years under his motto 'few, but ripe' — including a non-Euclidean geometry whose mathematics of curvature, through Riemann, later gave relativity its language. His name still marks the bell curve, a unit of magnetism, and the shape of curved space.

title: Charles Dickens
summary: The English novelist, born in 1812, who became one of the most popular and influential writers of the nineteenth century and made a whole age see itself. Pulled out of school as a boy to work in a blacking factory while his father sat in a debtors' prison, he turned the misery of industrial England into unforgettable stories. He published most of his great novels in cheap monthly or weekly instalments, building vast suspenseful plots read aloud in homes across Britain and beyond: Oliver Twist, A Christmas Carol, David Copperfield, Bleak House, Great Expectations. He fused comedy, sentiment, and biting social criticism, attacking the workhouse, the delays of the law, and industrial squalor, and gave the language a gallery of immortal characters whose name became an adjective. His art also carries shadows. Fagin, the villain of Oliver Twist, is drawn in crude antisemitic stereotype, though Dickens later softened the text and wrote a kindly Jewish character after Jewish readers objected. In 1858 he cast off his wife Catherine, mother of his ten children, in a cold public separation. He died in 1870, mourned across the English-speaking world.

title: Charlie Chaplin
summary: The English actor, director, writer, and composer who, as the bowler-hatted Tramp, became one of the most widely recognized people on earth and turned silent slapstick into a universal art. Born into Dickensian poverty in 1889 London, with a workhouse childhood and a mother lost to mental illness, Chaplin learned his craft in the music hall and then conquered the new medium of film. By the late 1910s the Tramp — cane, baggy trousers, toothbrush moustache, the dignity of the poor — had made him one of the first truly global movie stars, and he co-founded United Artists to control his own work. Films like The Kid, The Gold Rush, City Lights, and Modern Times fused breathtaking physical comedy with genuine pathos and social feeling; he clung to silence long after sound arrived, then spoke in The Great Dictator (1940) to satirize Hitler. His private life drew lasting scandal — a pattern of relationships with very young women, several marriages to teenagers, and a bruising 1940s paternity suit. Branded a communist sympathizer in the Red Scare, Chaplin, a lifelong British subject who never took US citizenship, was effectively barred from re-entering the United States in 1952 and settled in Switzerland. The country that exiled him gave him an honorary Oscar in 1972, to a long standing ovation. He died in 1977.

title: Claude Monet
summary: The French painter (1840–1926) who became the central figure of Impressionism, made working outdoors in changing light central to his art, while still building his canvases with deliberation, and spent his life trying to record not objects but the fleeting effects of sunlight and atmosphere. His painting Impression, Sunrise gave the movement its name, taken from a hostile critic's mockery. Through his series of haystacks, poplars, and Rouen Cathedral, and the vast Water Lilies of his garden at Giverny, he is widely seen as one of the painters who broke the long grip of realistic representation and helped open the door to modern art.

title: Dante Alighieri
summary: The Florentine poet (1265–1321) who, banished from his city for life under threat of being burned alive if he returned, wrote one of the greatest poems of the European Middle Ages in a highly crafted Tuscan vernacular rather than the Latin of the learned. In choosing the vernacular for the highest subject matter, and in making that poem the cornerstone of a literary tradition, he did more than any other early writer to make Tuscan the model for literary Italian, and he is held by tradition to be the father of the Italian language and one of the supreme poets of the Western canon.

title: Dmitri Mendeleev
summary: The Russian chemist who, in 1869, arranged the sixty-odd known elements by atomic weight into a single table whose recurring pattern he called the periodic law — and who set himself apart from others circling the same idea by leaving gaps for undiscovered elements and predicting their properties in detail. When gallium, scandium, and germanium were found within fifteen years, matching his forecasts with startling precision, the periodic table became the organizing map of chemistry. He worked without knowing why the pattern held; the deeper basis, atomic number, came only later through Moseley. Element 101, mendelevium, bears his name, though he never won the Nobel Prize.

title: Florence Nightingale
summary: The English reformer remembered as the gentle 'Lady with the Lamp' of the Crimean War, who was in fact a ferocious administrator and pioneering statistician — using mortality data and vivid charts to prove that most soldiers died of preventable disease, founding modern professional nursing, and helping to establish statistics as a tool of public-health reform.

title: Francisco Goya
summary: The Spanish painter (1746–1828) who rose from provincial Aragon to become first painter to the crown, then, after a severe illness left him deaf around 1792, turned his genius on war, superstition, and madness. His unflinching royal portraits, the satirical print series Los Caprichos, the war prints of The Disasters of War, the execution scene The Third of May 1808, and the nightmarish Black Paintings of his final years make him a hinge of art history: widely called both the last of the old masters and the first of the moderns.

title: Franz Kafka
summary: The German-speaking Jewish insurance official of Prague — legally trained but no courtroom lawyer — who, writing at night and publishing only a handful of short works, turned his private dread into stories of ordinary men crushed by faceless, incomprehensible power, and left a word for a feeling the whole twentieth century would learn. Born in 1883 in the Austro-Hungarian city of Prague, an outsider three times over, Kafka trained in law and worked, competently and miserably, as an insurance official. He was dominated by a domineering father, tormented by indecision over marriage, and dogged by illness. In his lifetime he published only a little, including The Metamorphosis, in which a man wakes transformed into a monstrous insect. His great novels, The Trial and The Castle, were unfinished and unpublished when he died of tuberculosis in 1924, at forty, little known outside a small literary circle. He asked his friend Max Brod to burn his manuscripts unread; Brod refused and published them, which is why we have his work at all. Though Kafka died before the catastrophe, all three of his sisters and his Czech translator and intimate Milena Jesenská were later murdered in the Nazi camps. He became one of the most influential writers of the century, and his name became an adjective, "Kafkaesque," for the nightmare of the powerless individual before an opaque and crushing authority.

title: Fyodor Dostoevsky
summary: The Russian novelist, born in 1821, who went down into the dark of the human soul and came back with God and the devil both. Arrested in 1849 for belonging to a radical circle, he was subjected to a mock execution, reprieved at the last second before the firing squad, then sent to a Siberian prison camp for four years. He came back a believing Orthodox Christian, an epileptic, and later a compulsive gambler perpetually in debt, writing at frantic speed against deadlines. Out of murder, suicide, faith, and doubt he made some of the deepest psychological novels in world literature: Crime and Punishment, The Idiot, Demons, and The Brothers Karamazov, with Notes from Underground a founding text of modern alienation. He wrote what the critic Bakhtin later called the polyphonic novel, letting clashing voices and ideas live with full force, and asked, more fiercely than anyone, whether life means anything if there is no God. Later readers condensed his fiction into the formula that if God is dead, everything is permitted; he never wrote it so neatly, but his novels return again and again to the fear that without God moral limits collapse. His shadow is real: in his later journalism, the Writer's Diary, the prophet of compassion was also a fervent Russian-Orthodox nationalist who voiced ugly antisemitism. He shaped modern psychology, existentialism, and the twentieth-century novel, and is ranked among the supreme novelists of all time.

title: George Orwell
summary: The English writer, born Eric Blair in 1903, who turned plain prose into a weapon for truth and gave the twentieth century its sharpest warnings against tyranny. He served as a colonial policeman in Burma, where he came to hate empire, then chose to live among the destitute and wrote it down in Down and Out in Paris and London. A committed democratic socialist, he went to fight fascism in the Spanish Civil War, was shot through the throat, and fled Spain after Soviet-backed Communists turned on rival anti-fascist leftists, a lifelong enemy thereafter of totalitarianism of every color. Two late books made him immortal: Animal Farm (1945), a fable of a revolution betrayed where 'all animals are equal, but some are more equal than others,' and Nineteen Eighty-Four (1949), a vision of total surveillance and thought control that named Big Brother, the Thought Police, Newspeak, and doublethink. In 'Politics and the English Language' he argued that vague, ugly words corrupt thought and serve power, making clear prose a moral cause. His record carries shadows: in 1949, dying, he gave a friend in a British government propaganda unit a list of writers he judged unreliable pro-Communist 'fellow-travelers,' an act that complicates his free-speech reputation, and he carried some prejudices of his era even as he attacked imperialism and antisemitism. He died of tuberculosis in 1950, at forty-six, and the word 'Orwellian' now names the abuse of power and language he spent his life exposing.

title: Gregor Mendel
summary: The Augustinian friar in Brno who, between roughly 1856 and 1863, bred and counted some 28,000 pea plants in a monastery garden and uncovered the hidden arithmetic of heredity — that traits pass not by blending but as discrete factors, now called genes, one inherited at random from each parent, with dominant forms masking recessive ones that reappear in a steady 3-to-1 ratio. His laws of segregation and independent assortment became foundational for genetics decades before anyone knew why they held. His 1866 paper was almost entirely ignored; only around 1900 did three botanists independently rediscover his work and launch 'Mendelism.' In 1936 the statistician R. A. Fisher noted his reported ratios fit theory unusually closely, a debate still unresolved but not generally taken to overturn his conclusions.

title: Hippocrates
summary: The Greek physician of Kos, remembered as the father of medicine, under whose name the roughly sixty texts of the Hippocratic Corpus gathered one of the earliest sustained insistences that disease has natural rather than divine causes — though almost nothing about the man can be securely known, the corpus was written by many hands over generations, and the famous Oath bearing his name cannot confidently be traced to him.

title: James Clerk Maxwell
summary: The Scottish physicist whose field equations — later streamlined into the four Maxwell equations taught today — united electricity, magnetism, and light as one electromagnetic phenomenon. Maxwell gave rigorous mathematical form to Michael Faraday's experimental picture of fields, showed that electricity and magnetism are two aspects of one phenomenon, and found that his equations predicted waves traveling at the speed of light — leading him to conclude that light itself is an electromagnetic wave. His theory foretold a whole spectrum of invisible waves, confirmed by Heinrich Hertz in 1887 after Maxwell's death, opening the way to radio. He also helped found statistical mechanics, produced the first durable three-color photograph, and is very widely ranked, with Newton and Einstein, among the greatest physicists who ever lived.

title: James Joyce
summary: The Irish writer (1882–1941) who left Dublin as a young man and spent his life in exile on the Continent, yet wrote about almost nothing but the city he had abandoned. Raised Catholic and Jesuit-educated, he broke with the Church and with Irish nationalism alike in the name of the free artist. In Dubliners and A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man, and above all in Ulysses, which retells Homer's Odyssey across a single ordinary Dublin day and pioneered the stream-of-consciousness rendering of the unfiltered mind, he pushed fiction further inward than anyone before him. Ulysses was condemned as obscene and suppressed in the United States and Britain for more than a decade; a 1933 American court ruling cleared it in the United States, with Britain following later. His final book, Finnegans Wake, dissolved language itself into multilingual dream-puns. A central figure of modernism, Joyce is ranked among the supreme novelists in English, though readers still divide over whether his late difficulty is genius or excess.

title: Jane Austen
summary: The English novelist, born in 1775, who proved that a few country families and the marriage market that ruled women's lives could hold an entire world. Writing quietly as a clergyman's daughter in Georgian and Regency England, never married and publishing anonymously (her first novel appeared "By a Lady"), she perfected irony and the inner view of character in six finished novels: Pride and Prejudice, Sense and Sensibility, Emma, Mansfield Park, Northanger Abbey, and Persuasion. Her great technical gift was free indirect discourse, slipping between the narrator's voice and a character's thoughts so that irony and sympathy work at once. Her comic, domestic surface covers a clear-eyed study of money, manners, and self-deception, and of how women's fates turned on a good marriage in a world that offered them little else. She died at forty-one, her fame still modest; it grew enormously after her death. Readers argue to this day over whether she is a quiet radical exposing women's constraints or a defender of the established gentry order, and scholars note how the wider world of war, empire, and slavery sits just offstage in her books. Either way she is among the most beloved and most studied novelists in English.

title: Johann Sebastian Bach
summary: The German composer who, working most of his life as a church and court musician far from any limelight, brought the art of weaving independent melodic lines into a single structure to one of its highest and most influential summits — and wrote music of such order and depth that it became a foundation of Western art-music training and contrapuntal thought since.

title: Johannes Kepler
summary: The German astronomer and mathematician who, trusting precise measurement over two thousand years of belief in perfect circles, discovered that the planets move in ellipses and wrote the three mathematical laws of planetary motion — turning astronomy into an exact science and laying the foundation Newton would later explain with gravity.

title: Leo Tolstoy
summary: The Russian novelist, born in 1828, who wrote with a god's-eye fullness of life and then turned from art to a radical Christianity that helped shape the modern idea of nonviolence. A wealthy count who fought as a young officer and then settled on his country estate, he produced the two vast novels often called the greatest of the nineteenth century: War and Peace, which follows aristocratic families through Napoleon's 1812 invasion of Russia and argues that history is made by countless small acts rather than great men, and Anna Karenina, which traces a married woman's ruinous love affair against the moral life of a whole society. Both fuse epic scale with a piercing psychological realism, and many rank him the supreme realist novelist, the master of fiction that seems simply to show the world as it is. In midlife he underwent a profound spiritual crisis and emerged a radical Christian moralist who rejected the Orthodox Church, the state, and private property, preached nonviolence and simplicity, and came to regard much art, including his own fiction, with deep suspicion. The Orthodox Church excommunicated him in 1901. Yet he remained a count, and the clash between his ideals and his life, and with his wife Sofya, was agonizing. His doctrine of nonviolent resistance to evil directly influenced Mohandas Gandhi. He died in 1910 as a fugitive from his own home, at a remote railway station.

title: Leonhard Euler
summary: The Swiss mathematician widely regarded as the most prolific in history, who reshaped the language and the agenda of mathematics across analysis, number theory, mechanics, optics, and astronomy. Working at the royal academies of St Petersburg and Berlin, Euler introduced or popularized much of the everyday notation students still use — f(x), e, i, the summation sign, and the modern symbol for pi — and produced so much that publication of his collected work continued for decades after his death. His solution to the puzzle of the seven bridges of Königsberg opened graph theory and later came to be seen as an ancestor of topology, and he kept pouring out first-rank mathematics even after he had gone nearly blind, aided by a remarkable memory and his sons as scribes.

title: Louis Pasteur
summary: The French chemist who became one of the founders of microbiology by showing that microscopic living things drive fermentation, spoilage, and disease. He demonstrated that gentle heating kills the microbes that sour liquids — pasteurization, still used on milk worldwide — and his swan-neck-flask experiments helped disprove spontaneous generation, the old belief that life arises from nonliving matter. His work was central to proving the germ theory of disease, though Robert Koch and others advanced it alongside him, often as rivals. Learning to weaken germs in the lab, he produced vaccines for chicken cholera, anthrax, and rabies, publicly launched through the 1885 survival of the boy Joseph Meister. His laboratory notebooks, opened to scholars by historian Gerald Geison in 1995, show that the public Pasteur sometimes differed from the private one, the messier reality behind a genuine genius whose work saved more lives than almost any other in science.

title: Michael Faraday
summary: The largely self-taught English experimentalist, born poor and apprenticed as a bookbinder, who discovered electromagnetic induction — the principle behind every electric generator and transformer — built the first primitive electric motor, found the laws of electrolysis linking electricity and chemistry, and gave physics the revolutionary idea of the field. Lacking advanced mathematics, Faraday pictured electric and magnetic forces as invisible lines of force filling space; James Clerk Maxwell later turned that picture into rigorous mathematics. His experiments laid the foundation of the electric age, and the field concept he introduced ran through Maxwell to Einstein, who reportedly kept a portrait of Faraday on his wall.

title: Michelangelo
summary: The Florentine sculptor, painter, and architect whose David, Pietà, and Sistine Chapel ceiling set a standard of human grandeur the West has measured itself against ever since — one of the supreme artists of the Italian Renaissance, who thought of himself first as a man who freed living figures from stone.

title: Miguel de Cervantes
summary: The Spanish writer (1547–1616) who, after a hard life of war, wounds, slavery, and prison, created in Don Quixote a deluded old knight and his earthy squire, and in doing so gave the world what is very widely called its first modern novel. He fought at the naval battle of Lepanto in 1571, where a wound left his left hand permanently maimed; he was captured by Barbary corsairs and spent five years enslaved in Algiers before he was ransomed. Back in Spain he worked as a tax collector, was jailed for debts, and knew poverty for most of his life. Late in that life he wrote Don Quixote, published in two parts (1605 and 1615), a hilarious satire of chivalric romance that is also a tender study of idealism and disillusion. The book broke from idealized romance into irony, realism, and rounded inner life, and even folded a rival's fake unauthorized sequel into its own pages. He is the towering figure of Spanish literature — Spanish is often called the language of Cervantes — and he died in 1616, the same year as Shakespeare.

title: Nicolaus Copernicus
summary: The Polish churchman and astronomer who, working quietly for decades, proposed that the Earth and planets orbit a central Sun and that the Earth spins daily on its axis — a sun-centered cosmos that displaced the Earth from its ancient place at the center and opened the revolution in astronomy that Kepler and Galileo would carry forward, even though Copernicus himself kept the perfect circles of the ancients and a model barely simpler than the one he replaced.

title: Niels Bohr
summary: The Danish physicist who in 1913 produced the first quantum model to explain the stability of hydrogen-like atoms and their spectral lines — electrons in fixed orbits, jumping between them in quantum leaps — and then became the presiding figure of quantum physics. From his institute in Copenhagen he developed the idea of complementarity and the interpretation of quantum mechanics that bears the city's name, and answered Einstein's objections over the nature of reality in debates that were never settled. Of partly Jewish descent, he escaped occupied Denmark in 1943, advised the Allied atomic bomb project, and then pressed Roosevelt and Churchill, in vain, to share nuclear knowledge openly before an arms race began. He spent his last years working for an 'open world.' He is ranked with Einstein among the founders of modern physics, and the element bohrium bears his name.

title: Rembrandt
summary: The Dutch painter and etcher (1606–1669) who became one of the supreme masters of light and shadow in the art of the Golden Age, turning biblical drama and ordinary faces into studies of inner life. A miller's son who rose to be one of Amsterdam's most sought-after portraitists, painter of The Night Watch, and one of the greatest printmakers in history, he painted himself across forty years in roughly eighty self-portraits, leaving the most honest record of a single human face in Western art. His later life was marked by the deaths of his wife and children, a church censure of his partner, and bankruptcy, while his work grew only deeper.

title: Stephen Hawking
summary: The British theoretical physicist who, paralyzed for most of his life by motor neurone disease, reshaped the understanding of black holes and the origin of the universe. Working where Einstein's gravity meets quantum physics, Hawking with Roger Penrose showed that, under classical general relativity, an expanding universe traced backward leads to a singularity where the theory breaks down, and then argued that black holes are not perfectly black but slowly glow and evaporate — a result known as Hawking radiation. That radiation has never been directly observed, because for any real black hole it is far too faint to detect, and the absence of confirmed evidence is why he never received a Nobel Prize. Through his book A Brief History of Time and a computer-synthesized voice that became world-famous, he turned cosmology into a public passion and became one of the most recognizable scientists of his age.

title: The Beatles
summary: The band from Liverpool — John Lennon, Paul McCartney, George Harrison, and Ringo Starr — that remade popular music in seven years. Between 1960 and 1970 the Beatles helped make pop singles and rock albums widely treated as serious art. They sharpened their act in the clubs of Hamburg, exploded into the screaming crowds of Beatlemania, and led the 1964 British Invasion of America. Driven by the Lennon-McCartney songwriting partnership, they grew with astonishing speed from simple love songs into complex, experimental music, turning the recording studio into an instrument on albums like Revolver and Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band. They are very widely regarded as the most influential band in the history of popular music, though their fame, their 1966 'bigger than Jesus' controversy, and their 1970 breakup all carry contested meanings.

title: Victor Hugo
summary: The French writer, born in 1802, who led the Romantic revolt against classical rules, poured out poems, plays, and vast novels, and became so much the conscience and the voice of his nation that a million people followed his coffin. He was the dominant literary figure of nineteenth-century France: poet, playwright, novelist, and politician. His 1830 play Hernani touched off the uproar known as the Battle of Hernani, between young Romantics and old guardians of the rules. He wrote two of the world's most beloved novels: The Hunchback of Notre-Dame, which made medieval Paris and its cathedral live again and helped revive public concern for the neglected building, and Les Misérables, the saga of the ex-convict Jean Valjean, the implacable Inspector Javert, and the Paris poor, a vast plea for mercy and social justice. He fought against the death penalty and poverty and for public education. When Louis-Napoleon seized power in 1851, Hugo denounced him and went into nineteen years of exile, mostly on the island of Guernsey, refusing to return until the emperor fell. By his own design he was also a monument to himself, vain, prolific, and endlessly self-dramatizing, and late in life he turned to spiritualism and the séance table. When he died in 1885, France gave him a state funeral and a vast crowd carried him to the Panthéon. His name is on streets and squares across the country, and the whole world still reads, stages, and films his stories.

title: Vincent van Gogh
summary: The Dutch painter (1853–1890) who came to art late, worked in a feverish rush of barely a decade, and used surging color and visible brushstroke to express anguish, hope, and intensity. Almost unknown in his lifetime and dependent on his devoted brother Theo, he painted the Sunflowers, the cypresses, the Starry Night, and his haunted self-portraits, pushing past Impressionism into pure, vivid color. He suffered severe mental illness whose exact nature is still debated, was voluntarily institutionalized at Saint-Rémy, and died at thirty-seven of a gunshot wound generally regarded as suicide. A central figure of Post-Impressionism and a forerunner of Expressionism, he sold almost nothing while he lived, and became, after his early death, one of the most loved and influential painters in history.

title: Virgil
summary: The supreme poet of the Latin language (70–19 BC), who lived through the violent collapse of the Roman Republic into the empire of Augustus and wrote, in his masterwork the Aeneid, the national epic that gave Rome a heroic founding myth and a sense of destiny. The Aeneid is at once one of the greatest poems of the West and, in part, a work that served Augustus's new order, since Augustus claimed descent from its hero, Aeneas. Virgil's polished, melancholy hexameters set the standard for epic and for Latin itself; he became so revered that medieval Christians read him as a pagan prophet of Christ, and Dante made him his guide through Hell and Purgatory.

title: Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart
summary: The Austrian composer who toured Europe as a child prodigy and then, in a short and crowded life, poured out operas, symphonies, and concertos of such grace, balance, and feeling that he became the very model of natural musical genius — and whose early death in 1791 bred the lasting myths of a poisoning rival and a pauper's grave, neither of which the evidence supports.

title: Amsterdam
summary: The Dutch port built on a drained marsh below sea level — which ringed itself with planned canals around 1600, became one of the world's richest and most important trading cities during the seventeenth-century Golden Age, hosted the first modern stock exchange and the home base of the Dutch East India Company, drew refugees with its relative tolerance and funded the painters of the age, and rested its fortune partly on colonial conquest and the Atlantic slave trade.

title: Berlin
summary: The city on the marshy Spree that staged the twentieth century — the Prussian seat that became capital of a united Germany in 1871, then the command post of Nazism, the capital from which the already-begun genocide of Europe's Jews was coordinated, then the front line of the Cold War split in two by a wall from 1961 to 1989, and now the reunited capital of a democracy that lives among its own ruins and memorials.

title: Florence
summary: The Tuscan banking town on the Arno that, in two extraordinary centuries, gave the world Dante's language, Renaissance art and architecture, the banking and credit practices behind modern finance, and Machiavelli's politics — a city of perhaps fifty thousand people that is remembered, above all, as the cradle of the Renaissance.

title: London
summary: The tidal-river port the Romans founded as Londinium around 47 CE, which grew into the capital of the largest empire in history and one of the great engines of modern finance, law, the English language, and literature — a metropolis whose splendor was built on industry and on the profits of empire and the Atlantic slave trade, and which survived the loss of both to remake itself as a global financial center and one of the world's most diverse cities.

title: Moscow
summary: The fortress city on the Moskva river that rose under Mongol overlordship to gather the Russian lands around its Kremlin, claimed for itself the title of a third Rome and last seat of Orthodox Christianity, burned to deny Napoleon his victory, and became the capital of the Soviet Union and the command center of its terror.

title: Paris
summary: The capital on the Seine that grew from a Roman river town into one of Europe's great centers of learning and Enlightenment argument, a central stage of modern revolution, and a self-appointed capital of art and beauty — a splendor repeatedly written in blood, from the Terror's guillotine to Haussmann's displacements to the Nazi occupation and the 1942 roundup of its Jews.

title: St Petersburg
summary: The city Russia willed into being on a Baltic marsh — founded by Peter the Great in 1703 by forced labor, raised as a planned imperial capital of palaces and canals to face Europe, made the cradle of the 1917 revolution, and renamed Leningrad to endure a German siege that starved roughly a million people.

title: The Hanseatic League
summary: The loose league of German-speaking trading towns that, for some four centuries without a king or a standing army of its own, dominated the key trade routes and privileges of the Baltic and North Sea, set the terms of northern Europe's trade, and showed how merchants alone could wield the power of a state.

title: The Republic of Letters
summary: The borderless community of scholars and thinkers who, from roughly 1500 to 1800, built a 'republic' with no land and no laws, bound only by a shared commitment to exchanging knowledge by letter across the lines of nation and religion. Carried by the post, the printing press, learned journals, and a common scholarly tongue, it became the circulatory system through which the findings of the new science and the arguments of the age of reason moved across Europe. It claimed to be open and equal, judged only by ideas, yet in practice it was dominated by educated, propertied men, with women and the poor largely shut out — a real and powerful network that was also, in part, a flattering self-image.

title: Vienna
summary: The Danube city that was the seat of the Habsburg dynasty and the gate at which Europe twice turned back the Ottomans — and that, across two centuries, became one of the supreme capitals of Western music and, in its dazzling final glow around 1900, produced modern thought and modern art, even as it bred the antisemitism that would help consume it in the Nazi annexation of 1938 and the Holocaust.

title: Faust
summary: Johann Wolfgang von Goethe's two-part dramatic poem (Part One 1808, Part Two 1832), worked on across some sixty years — often called the supreme work of German literature, in which a restless scholar who has exhausted human knowledge makes a pact with the devil Mephistopheles, wagering his soul for boundless experience. Goethe reworked an older legend in which Faust is damned; his Faust, controversially, is saved. The poem became the central modern myth of striving and its price, and gave the language the phrase 'Faustian bargain.'

title: Frankenstein
summary: Mary Shelley's 1818 novel, begun when she was eighteen on a ghost-story dare at Lake Geneva, in which a young scientist named Victor Frankenstein assembles a living being from dead flesh and then, horrified, flees from it. Often called the first modern science-fiction novel, it is also the modern world's deepest parable about the things we create and then refuse to take responsibility for. The creature it made famous is the maker's victim, not a brute, and the name 'Frankenstein' belongs to the scientist, not the monster he abandons.

title: Greek Tragedy
summary: The dramatic form that emerged in archaic Athens and flourished in democratic Athens in the 5th century BC, performed at the festivals of the god Dionysus — staged in public competition before thousands of citizens, acted by masked men and a singing chorus, in which great figures are destroyed by fate, the gods, and their own flaws. It helped create the Western theatrical tradition, gave us the idea of tragedy itself, and through Aristotle's later theory of catharsis shaped two thousand years of thinking about why we watch suffering.

title: Hamlet
summary: Shakespeare's tragedy of a grieving prince (written around 1600), commanded by his father's ghost to avenge a murder, who instead questions, doubts, and delays — the longest and most famous of his plays, and the work most often credited with putting a fully modern inner life on the stage. In his soliloquies, above all 'To be, or not to be,' a character first seems to think aloud and to possess a hidden self. Why Hamlet delays has never been settled, and the play survives in several differing early texts, with no single original.
aliases: The Tragedy of Hamlet, Prince of Denmark
indexTerms: To be or not to be

title: Newton's Principia
summary: The 1687 book in which Isaac Newton showed that the same simple mathematical laws govern a falling apple and the orbiting moon — uniting heaven and earth under one system of motion and gravity, and founding the science of physics.

title: Opera
period: Born in Italy c. 1597–1600; matured through Monteverdi and the national styles; a living art performed in great houses worldwide today
summary: The art form, born in Italy around 1600, that tries to do everything at once: it fuses music, singing, drama, poetry, staging, and spectacle into a single overwhelming experience in which characters do not speak but sing. Opera is sung drama carried by an orchestra, weaving recitative (sung speech that moves the plot) with the aria (the showpiece song of emotion), the chorus, and the overture. It grew from a Florentine circle, the Camerata, who tried to revive what they imagined was ancient Greek sung drama; Monteverdi made it great art, and it spread across Europe into the Italian bel canto, the German music-drama, and the French grand opera, through composers including Mozart, Rossini, Verdi, Wagner, and Puccini. Its glory had costs: for roughly two centuries its most prized singers were the castrati, boys castrated to preserve a high, powerful voice, a celebrated but cruel practice now long abolished. Hugely expensive to stage and long tied to wealth, opera has always drawn the charge of elitism, yet at its height it was also among the most popular of arts, and it endures in great houses worldwide.

title: The Gothic Cathedral
summary: The great church form that arose in northern France around 1140 and spread across Europe by 1500. Its signature is verticality and light: the pointed arch, the ribbed vault, and the external flying buttress work together to carry the roof's weight on a skeleton of stone, freeing the walls to become vast windows of stained glass. A cathedral was the work of generations, often a century or more, built by guilds of masons and funded by the Church, towns, guilds, and pilgrims, and many were never finished as first planned. It displayed the wealth and power of the medieval Church as much as devotion, and remains among the supreme achievements of European architecture.

title: The Mona Lisa
period: begun c. 1503; worked on until c. 1519
summary: A small oil portrait painted by Leonardo da Vinci, begun around 1503 in Florence and worked on for years. It shows a woman, most likely Lisa del Giocondo, the wife of a silk merchant, seated before a hazy landscape with a faint, ambiguous smile. The painting is a masterpiece of soft light and living expression, built with Leonardo's sfumato technique of smoke-like gradations with no hard lines. It was always admired, but its modern status as the most famous painting on earth was hugely amplified by an accident of history. Its theft from the Louvre in 1911 made it a global media sensation, and endless mass reproduction carried it the rest of the way. It now hangs behind glass in the Louvre, the most visited and reproduced artwork in history.

title: The Napoleonic Code
summary: The civil law code promulgated in France in 1804, drafted at Napoleon's direction to replace the country's tangle of feudal, regional, and Roman law with one clear written system. It gave ordinary men equality before the law, secure property, and freedom of contract, and it spread across much of the world as the model of modern civil law — even as it made married women legal dependents of their husbands and served a state that had restored slavery in the French colonies in 1802.

title: The Parthenon
summary: The great Doric marble temple of the goddess Athena, raised on the Athenian Acropolis between 447 and 432 BCE under the statesman Pericles, designed by Iktinos and Kallikrates with sculpture overseen by Phidias. Once it held a giant gold-and-ivory statue of Athena, and it is famous for its 'refinements' — columns that swell and lean and lines that gently curve, so the whole looks perfectly straight. The supreme surviving monument of classical Greek architecture, it became the emblem of Greece, of democracy, and of classical beauty, even though it was funded substantially from the treasury of the Delian League, the alliance Athens turned into an empire. Wrecked in 1687 when a Venetian shell hit Ottoman gunpowder stored inside, and stripped of about half its surviving sculptures by Lord Elgin around 1801–1812, it remains the most influential building in the Western tradition and the center of an unresolved argument over the Parthenon Marbles.

title: The Sistine Chapel Ceiling
summary: The vast fresco that Michelangelo painted between 1508 and 1512 for Pope Julius II, across roughly 500 square metres of the ceiling of the pope's chapel in the Vatican. Built around nine scenes from the Book of Genesis and crowded with hundreds of figures, it is crowned by the Creation of Adam, in which God reaches a finger toward the newly made man across a small, charged gap. Painted standing on scaffolding with his head bent back, not lying on his back, by a reluctant sculptor who never wanted the job, it became the most famous ceiling on earth and a pinnacle of the High Renaissance. Its controversial 1980s and 1990s cleaning revealed startlingly vivid color, a result scholars largely accepted after a public debate.

title: The Peace of Westphalia
summary: The set of treaties signed in 1648 in the German towns of Munster and Osnabruck that ended the Thirty Years' War and the Eighty Years' War — a vast multilateral settlement that redrew Europe's map, confirmed each ruler's right to set their territory's religion among Catholic, Lutheran, and Calvinist, and recognized the independence of the Dutch and the Swiss. Later memory turned it into the founding charter of the sovereign state and 'Westphalian sovereignty,' a reputation that historians now treat as a myth read backward onto the documents.

title: Modern Art
summary: Not a single style but an umbrella term for roughly a century of successive avant-garde movements, from the 1860s to the 1960s, in which Western artists abandoned the centuries-old goal of realistic representation and reinvented what a picture could be, again and again. Partly freed by photography from the job of recording the world, painters and sculptors turned to color, form, emotion, and idea. The chain ran from Impressionism through Post-Impressionism, Fauvism and Expressionism, Cubism, Dada and Surrealism, pure abstraction, and finally American Abstract Expressionism. It provoked a lasting popular backlash -- 'my child could do that,' 'is it even art?' -- and remade design, advertising, and how the modern eye sees, while opening a permanent gap between adventurous art and a wary public.

title: The Jesuits
summary: The Catholic religious order founded in 1540 as a disciplined force for the Counter-Reformation, which became the Church's most influential educators, missionaries, and intellectuals — feared and admired across the world, suppressed by their own Pope in 1773, and restored in 1814.

title: The League of Nations
summary: The first permanent international organization built to keep world peace, founded in 1920 after the First World War on the principle of collective security. Headquartered in Geneva and eventually some sixty members strong, it pioneered cooperation on health, labor, and refugees while proving too weak to stop the aggressors of the 1930s. The United States never joined, the League had no army, and it collapsed into a second world war — yet it became the blueprint for the United Nations.

title: The Green Revolution
summary: The mid-twentieth-century transformation of farming, from roughly the 1940s to the 1970s, that spread high-yield varieties of wheat and rice, synthetic fertilizer, and intensive irrigation across the developing world. Where it took hold, harvests doubled or tripled, and the new methods are widely credited with averting predicted famines and saving hundreds of millions, perhaps a billion, from starvation. The same methods also drained groundwater, degraded soil, narrowed crop diversity, and favored the larger farmers who could afford the inputs, leaving a dual legacy still debated today.

title: The Human Genome Project
summary: The thirteen-year international effort, from 1990 to 2003, to read all three billion chemical letters of human DNA and map its genes — the largest collaborative biology project ever attempted. A publicly funded consortium spanning the United States, United Kingdom, France, Germany, Japan, and China was challenged midway by a private company, Celera, led by Craig Venter, in a race over whether the genome should be free or owned. The finished sequence gave medicine a reference text for the species, founded the field of genomics, and crashed the cost of reading DNA. It also revealed how much harder the genome would be to read than to spell, and raised lasting questions about genetic privacy, discrimination, and the patenting of human genes.

title: The Moon Landing
